records.ety: [OE. herbor, herberwe, herberge, Icel. herbergi (cf. OHG. heriberga), orig., a shelter for soldiers; herr army + bjarga to save, help, defend; akin to AS. here army, G. heer, OHG. heri, Goth. harjis, and AS. beorgan to save, shelter, defend, G. bergen. See Harry, 2d Bury, and cf. Harbinger.]

defs: A station for rest and entertainment; a place of security and comfort; a refuge; a shelter.
quotes: [A grove] fair harbour that them seems.
For harbor at a thousand doors they knocked.

defs: Specif.: A lodging place; an inn.
The mansion of a heavenly body.
A portion of a sea, a lake, or other large body of water, either landlocked or artificially protected so as to be a place of safety for vessels in stormy weather; a port or haven.

records.ety: [OE. herberen, herberwen, herbergen; cf. Icel. herbergja. See Harbor, n.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Harbored (-brd); p. pr. & vb. n. Harboring.]

defs: To afford lodging to; to entertain as a guest; to shelter; to receive; to give a refuge to; to indulge or cherish (a thought or feeling, esp. an ill thought); as, to harbor a grudge.
quotes: The bare suspicion made it treason to harbor the person suspected.
Let not your gentle breast harbor one thought of outrage.

defs: To lodge, or abide for a time; to take shelter, as in a harbor.
quotes: For this night let's harbor here in York.
